title: Can the planet's coral reefs be saved?
content_html:
Can the planet’s coral reefs be saved?

Can the planet’s coral reefs be saved?

A.

Conservationists have put the final touches to a giant artificial reef they have been assembling at the world-renowned Zoological Society of London (London Zoo). Samples of the planet’s most spectacular corals - vivid green branching coral, yellow scroll, blue ridge and many more species - have been added to the giant tank along with fish that thrive in their presence: blue tang, clownfish and many others. The reef is in the zoo’s new gallery, Tiny Giants, which is dedicated to the minuscule invertebrate creatures that sustain life across the planet. The coral reef tank and its seven-metre-wide window form the core of the exhibition. ’Coral reefs are the most diverse ecosystems on Earth and we want to show people how wonderful they are,’ said Paul Pearce-Kelly, senior curator of invertebrates and fish at the Zoological Society of London. ’However, we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts that are now being carried out to try to save them from the threat of global warming.’ They want people to see what is being done to try to save these wonders.

B.

Corals are composed of tiny animals, known as polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures in the sea water. These polyps are transparent but get their brilliant tones of pink, orange, blue, green, etc. From algae that live within them, which in turn get protection, while their photosynthesising of the sun’s rays provides nutrients for the polyps. This comfortable symbiotic relationship has led to the growth of coral reefs that cover 0.1% of the planet’s ocean bed while providing homes for more than 25% of marine species, including fish, molluscs, sponges and shellfish.

C.

As a result, coral reefs are often described as the ’rainforests of the sea’, though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists, including David Attenborough. ’People say you cannot beat the rainforest,’ Attenborough has stated. ’But that is simply not true. You go there and the first thing you think is: where ... are the birds? Where are the animals? They are hiding in the trees, of course. No, if you want beauty and wildlife, you want a coral reef. Put on a mask and stick your head under the water. The sight is mind-blowing.’

D.

Unfortunately, these majestic sights are now under very serious threat, with the most immediate problem coming in the form of thermal stress. Rising ocean temperatures are triggering bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour and eventually kill them. And that is just the start. Other menaces include ocean acidification, sea level increase, pollution by humans, deoxygenation and ocean current changes, while the climate crisis is also increasing habitat destruction. As a result, vast areas - including massive chunks of Australia’s Great Barrier Reef - have already been destroyed, and scientists advise that more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050 unless urgent action is taken to tackle global heating and greenhouse gas emissions. Pearce-Kelly says that coral reefs have to survive really harsh conditions – wave erosion and other factors. And ’when things start to go wrong in the oceans, then corals will be the first to react. And that is exactly what we are seeing now. Coral reefs are dying and they are telling us that all is not well with our planet.’

E.

However, scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral that could survive our overheated oceans, and some of this research will be carried out at London Zoo. ’Behind our ... coral reef tank we have built laboratories where scientists will be studying coral species,’ said Pearce-Kelly. One aim will be to carry out research on species to find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters. Another will be to try to increase coral breeding rates. ’Coral spawn just once a year,’ he added. ’However, aquarium-based research has enabled some corals to spawn artificially, which can assist coral reef restoration efforts. And if this can be extended for all species, we could consider the launching of coral-spawning programmes several times a year. That would be a big help in restoring blighted reefs.

F.

Research in these fields is being conducted in laboratories around the world, with the London Zoo centre linked to this global network. Studies carried out in one centre can then be tested in others. The resulting young coral can then be displayed in the tank in Tiny Giants. ’The crucial point is that the progress we make in making coral better able to survive in a warming world can be shown to the public and encourage them to believe that we can do something to save the planet’s reefs,’ said Pearce-Kelly. ’Saving our coral reefs is now a critically important ecological goal.’

questions.question: Section A
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 14–19

Reading Passage has paragraphs A–E. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph from the list of headings below.

Write the correct number, i–vii, in boxes 14–19 on your answer sheet.
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
London Zoo
exhibition
show people
conservation efforts
Bước 2: Tìm ý chính đoạn A

we want to show people how wonderful they are

we also want to highlight the research and conservation efforts

Bước 3: Phân tích

Đoạn A có 2 mục tiêu giáo dục:

Cho khách tham quan thấy vẻ đẹp của san hô.
Cho họ biết các nỗ lực bảo tồn san hô.
=> Hai mục tiêu rõ ràng.
 v. Two clear educational goals

headings: Disagreement about the accuracy of a certain phrase
A warning of further trouble ahead

- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

questions.questionHtml:
List of Headings

  1. Tried and tested solutions
  2. Cooperation beneath the waves
  3. Working to lessen the problems
  4. Disagreement about the accuracy of a certain phrase
  5. Two clear educational goals
  6. Promoting hope
  7. A warning of further trouble ahead

14_______  Paragraph A

15_______  Paragraph B

16_______  Paragraph C

17_______  Paragraph D

18_______  Paragraph E

questions.question: Section B
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
polyps
algae
protection
nutrients
Bước 2: Tìm ý chính

algae live within them

algae get protection

algae provide nutrients for the polyps

Bước 3: Phân tích

San hô và tảo cùng có lợi cho nhau:

Tảo được bảo vệ.
San hô nhận dinh dưỡng.

=> Quan hệ cộng sinh.
15 = ii. Cooperation beneath the waves

questions.question: Section C
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
rainforests of the sea
dismissed
Attenborough
Bước 2: Tìm ý chính

coral reefs are often described as the rainforests of the sea

though the comparison is dismissed by some naturalists

Bước 3: Phân tích

Đoạn này nói về việc có người không đồng ý với cách gọi đó.

 iv. Disagreement about the accuracy of a certain phrase

questions.question: Section D
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
threat
bleaching
acidification
lost by 2050
Bước 2: Tìm ý chính

more than 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050

Bước 3: Phân tích

Đoạn nói hàng loạt mối đe dọa hiện tại và tương lai.

=> Cảnh báo tình hình còn tệ hơn nữa.

 vii. A warning of further trouble ahead

questions.question: Section E
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
scientists
survive
research
breeding
Bước 2: Tìm ý chính

scientists are trying to pinpoint hardy types of coral

increase coral breeding rates

Bước 3: Phân tích

Đoạn mô tả các biện pháp giảm thiểu thiệt hại và phục hồi san hô.

 iii. Working to lessen the problems

questions.question: Section F
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
encourage
believe
save the planet's reefs
Bước 2: Tìm ý chính

encourage them to believe that we can do something

Bước 3: Phân tích

Thông điệp cuối cùng là vẫn còn hy vọng cứu san hô.

vi. Promoting hope

questions.text: Which TWO of these causes of damage to coral reefs are mentioned by the writer of the text?
options: a rising number of extreme storms
the removal of too many fish from the sea
the contamination of the sea from waste
increased disease among marine species
alterations in the usual flow of water in the seas
questions.question: Which TWO of these causes of damage to coral reefs are mentioned by the writer of the text?
questions.explanation:
rising number of extreme storms X  (không đề cập rõ)
the removal of too many fish X
the contamination of the sea from waste ✔ (pollution by humans)
increased disease among marine species X
alterations in sea current flow 
Bước 1: Keyword
“damage causes”, “threats”, “pollution”, “currents”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn D
Bước 3: Phân tích
“pollution by humans” = contamination 
“ocean current changes” 
không nhắc storms, overfishing, disease

questions.text: Which TWO of the following statements are true of the researchers at London Zoo?
options: They are hoping to expand the numbers of different corals being bred in laboratories
They want to identify corals that can cope well with the changed sea conditions.
They are looking at ways of creating artificial reefs that corals could grow on.
They are trying out methods that would speed up reproduction in some corals.
They are investigating materials that might protect reefs from higher temperatures.
questions.question: Which TWO of the following statements are true of the researchers at London Zoo?
questions.explanation:
Bước 1: Keyword
“researchers London Zoo”, “breeding”, “survive”, “lab”
Bước 2: Tìm đoạn văn
Đoạn E
Bước 3: Phân tích
“find those that can survive best in warm, acidic waters” ✔
“increase coral breeding rates” ✔
“spawn artificially” = speed reproduction ✔
không nói artificial reef materials X

questions.text: Corals have a number of  ................... which they use to collect their food.
questions.question: Corals have a number of  ................... which they use to collect their food.
questions.groupHtml:
Questions 24–26

Complete the notes below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“Corals have a number of ___ which they use to collect their food.”

Đoạn B:
“polyps, with tentacles for capturing small marine creatures”

→ collect food = capture creatures

→ Đáp án: tentacles.

_blockConfig.noteContent:
- Corals have a number of [24] which they use to collect their food.
- Algae gain [25] from being inside the coral.
- Increases in the warmth of the sea water can remove the [26] from coral.

questions.questionHtml:
• Corals have a number of 24_______ which they use to collect their food.
  • Algae gain 25_______ from being inside the coral.
  • Increases in the warmth of the sea water can remove the 26_______ from coral.

questions.text: Algae gain ................from being inside the coral.
questions.question: Algae gain ................from being inside the coral.
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“Algae gain ___ from being inside the coral.”

Đoạn:
“algae ... get protection”

→ Đáp án: protection.

questions.text: Increases in the warmth of the sea water can remove the  ................... from coral.
questions.question: Increases in the warmth of the sea water can remove the  ................... from coral.
questions.explanation:
Câu:
“Increases in the warmth of the sea water can remove the ___ from coral.”

Đoạn D:
“bleaching events that strip reefs of their colour”

→ remove the colour = strip of colour

→ Đáp án: colour.
